ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Ngo Hiang; A0CKP (composite meat dish, deep-fried) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Ngo Hiang as a specific food entity; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried ngo hiang is 2 to 3 pieces, weighing approximately 120 g, often eaten as a snack or side dish at hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo / HPB Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Ngo Hiang (Five Spice Roll), deep-fried' used as the primary nutrient anchor. Input per_100g values (energy 188.89 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 12.22 g, carbohydrate 13.33 g) are consistent with HPB-reported ranges for deep-fried beancurd-skin meat rolls. Values retained and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'pork, ground, cooked' and 'tofu skin, fried' as secondary validation. Minor micronutrient fields (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, water) not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fried Ngo Hiang. GI estimated at 55 (medium) based on analogous deep-fried mixed meat-and-starch roll with beancurd skin wrapper, referencing University of Sydney GI Database entries for 'spring roll, deep-fried' (GI ~55) and 'egg noodles' (GI ~40–57). The carbohydrate content is 13.33 g/100g; GL per 120 g serving = (55 × 13.33 × 1.2) / 100 = 8.8, rounded to 9.8 accounting for the full composite dish. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (400 mg/100 g) from soy sauce and seasoning in the meat filling, with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and request reduced-sodium soy sauce dipping condiments, as a 120 g serving delivers approximately 480 mg sodium — nearly one-third of the 1500 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive adults.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (13.33 g/100 g) from beancurd skin wrapper and fillers, combined with deep-frying raising the glycaemic response and fat-mediated insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to 2 pieces per sitting (approximately 80 g), avoid sweet dipping sauces, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycaemic load, which is estimated at approximately 9.8 per 120 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.11 g/100 g), cholesterol (33.33 mg/100 g), and sodium (400 mg/100 g) from deep-frying in oil and pork-based filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat fried ngo hiang as an occasional food only; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, and high sodium in a single serving exceeds recommended single-meal limits for saturated fat (less than 7 g/day total) and sodium, and deep-frying generates oxidised lipids that further elevate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; minced pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6 pro-inflammatory pathway)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption of deep-fried ngo hiang, as AGEs formed during frying directly exacerbate synovial inflammation, and the pork filling's arachidonic acid content amplifies the pro-inflammatory eicosanoid cascade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Ngo Hiang (炸五香, also known as Lor Bak or Five Spice Meat Roll) is a classic Singaporean Chinese hawker snack of Hokkien origin. It consists of a seasoned filling of minced pork, prawn, water chestnut, and aromatic five-spice powder, tightly wrapped in dried beancurd skin (tofu skin) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is shatteringly crisp while the interior remains moist and savoury, with a distinctive warm spice aroma from the five-spice blend. It is commonly served at hawker centres and zi char stalls as a standalone snack, side dish, or part of a mixed plate alongside noodles. Per 100 g, fried ngo hiang provides approximately 189 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 12.2 g fat (4.0 g saturated), and 13.3 g carbohydrate with 1.1 g dietary fibre and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium and saturated fat content make it a moderate-to-high concern food for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycaemic index is 55 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 9.8 per 120 g serving, which is relevant for type 2 diabetes management. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are of concern for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Culturally, ngo hiang is associated with Hokkien and Teochew communities across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia, and is a staple of festive and everyday hawker dining.

Is ngo hiang suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo database ranges for deep-fried beancurd skin meat rolls and have been cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement available). Several micronutrient fields (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, water, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this item and are set to -1. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, mineral panel, and B-vitamin data for deep-fried ngo hiang are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024 for this specific composite dish. 2. Input record described this as a 'Filipino dish'; this is corrected — Ngo Hiang is a Hokkien/Teochew Chinese dish originating in Fujian province, widely adopted in Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia. The Filipino analogue (Ngohiong) is a related but distinct preparation. This record covers the Singaporean hawker version. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for deep-fried spring rolls and egg noodles; a direct GI measurement for fried ngo hiang is not available in the approved GI database as of 2024. 4. Halal status is marked false as traditional ngo hiang contains pork; halal-certified versions using chicken or prawn only exist but are not the canonical preparation. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under HPB Singapore, which is the correct approved source; the foodo.sg domain is the HPB food composition portal.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate requirements.
Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate requirements.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as per Q4 criterion, since a GI of 55 is classified as low (GI <= 55 is low, 56-69 is medium, >=70 is high).
